questions.question:
Packets of the same session may be routed through different paths in:
A. TCP, but not UDP
B. TCP and UDP
C. UDP, but not TCP
D. Neither TCP nor UDP
questions.to_find: Which transport protocol(s) allow packets of the same session to be routed through different paths

understand.plain: Both TCP and UDP are transport layer protocols that rely on the IP layer below them for actual packet routing. IP is a connectionless, best-effort protocol that routes each packet independently. This means that even packets belonging to the same TCP or UDP session can travel via different network paths and arrive out of order.

keywords.explain: The IP layer makes an independent routing decision for every packet, with no guarantee that consecutive packets take the same path.
keywords.example: Packet 1 of a TCP stream may go via router A-B-C while packet 2 goes via A-D-C.

keywords.explain: A logical end-to-end communication between two hosts identified by source IP, destination IP, source port, and destination port.
keywords.example: A single HTTP download is one TCP session, but its IP packets can still take different routes.

understand.visual_alt: At the transport layer, both TCP and UDP hand packets down to IP. IP routes each datagram independently, so any session's packets can follow different paths through the network.

given.aim: Determine for which transport protocol(s) same-session packets may be routed through different paths.

terms.connects: TCP provides reliability at the transport layer but does NOT control routing; IP underneath still routes packets independently.
UDP also hands packets to IP, which routes each independently.

terms.meaning: Each IP datagram is routed independently based on current routing table state.
terms.example: Router updates can change the path mid-session.
terms.connects: This is the root reason packets of ANY session can take different paths, regardless of transport protocol.

given.plan: Both TCP and UDP operate above IP. IP is connectionless and routes every datagram independently, so packets of the same session — whether TCP or UDP — can follow different paths. The answer is both TCP and UDP.

steps.title: Identify the layer responsible for routing
steps.apply: Routing is done by the Network layer (IP), not the transport layer (TCP/UDP). Both TCP and UDP pass their segments as payloads inside IP datagrams.
steps.note: Transport protocols have no control over how IP routes individual packets.

steps.title: Apply IP's best-effort, connectionless property
steps.apply: IP routes each datagram independently. Even consecutive packets sent by the same TCP connection or UDP socket can be forwarded via different routers if the routing tables change between packets. Therefore, both TCP sessions and UDP sessions can have their packets routed through different paths.
steps.note: TCP guarantees in-order delivery by resequencing at the receiver, which masks the fact that out-of-order delivery can occur due to different paths.

solution.result: Both TCP and UDP can have their packets routed through different paths. Answer: B. TCP and UDP.

questions.formula_note: IP connectionless routing: every datagram is forwarded independently, so path diversity exists for both TCP and UDP sessions.

questions.question:
The transport layer protocols used for real time multimedia, file transfer, DNS and email, respectively are
A. UDP, TCP, UDP and TCP
B. TCP, UDP, TCP and UDP
C. UDP, TCP, TCP and UDP
D. TCP, UDP, UDP and TCP
questions.answer: A. UDP, TCP, UDP and TCP
questions.to_find: The correct mapping of transport protocols to applications: real-time multimedia, file transfer, DNS, email

understand.plain: Different applications choose TCP or UDP based on their requirements. Real-time apps (video/audio streaming) prefer UDP because low latency is more important than reliability. File transfer needs TCP for guaranteed, in-order delivery. DNS typically uses UDP for fast, short queries. Email (SMTP) uses TCP for reliable message delivery.

keywords.explain: Connectionless, low-overhead protocol with no retransmission, suitable where speed matters more than reliability.
keywords.example: DNS queries, VoIP, live video streaming use UDP.

keywords.explain: Connection-oriented, reliable protocol with flow and congestion control, suitable for data that must arrive completely and in order.
keywords.example: FTP (file transfer), SMTP (email), HTTP use TCP.

understand.visual_alt: Table mapping: Real-time multimedia -> UDP, File transfer (FTP) -> TCP, DNS -> UDP, Email (SMTP) -> TCP.

given.aim: Match each application type to its transport protocol: real-time multimedia, file transfer, DNS, email.

terms.example: Online video calls, live TV streaming.
terms.connects: Uses UDP: occasional packet loss is tolerable but retransmission delay is not.

terms.meaning: Moving a complete file from one host to another (e.g., FTP).
terms.example: Downloading a software installer via FTP.
terms.connects: Uses TCP: the file must arrive complete and intact.

terms.meaning: Domain Name System — translates hostnames to IP addresses.
terms.connects: Uses UDP: queries are small, fast responses desired; timeout-and-retry handles rare failures.

terms.example: Sending a message via Gmail's SMTP server.
terms.connects: Uses TCP: email content must be delivered reliably and completely.

given.plan: Recall standard protocol-to-application mappings: multimedia -> UDP, FTP -> TCP, DNS -> UDP, SMTP -> TCP. Match to answer options.

steps.title: Determine protocol for each application
steps.apply:
Real-time multimedia (VoIP, streaming): UDP — latency-sensitive, tolerates loss.
File transfer (FTP): TCP — must deliver all data reliably.
DNS: UDP — small query/response, speed preferred, runs over port 53/UDP.
Email (SMTP): TCP — reliable delivery of message data required.
steps.note: DNS also supports TCP for zone transfers and large responses, but standard queries use UDP.

steps.title: Match to answer options
steps.apply:
The sequence is: UDP, TCP, UDP, TCP.
Option A: UDP, TCP, UDP and TCP — matches perfectly.
steps.note: Options B and D start with TCP for multimedia, which is incorrect. Option C gives TCP for DNS, which is non-standard for regular queries.

solution.result: Real-time multimedia: UDP, File transfer: TCP, DNS: UDP, Email: TCP. Answer: A. UDP, TCP, UDP and TCP.

questions.formula_note: Application-to-transport protocol mapping: latency-sensitive/loss-tolerant apps use UDP; reliability-requiring apps use TCP.

questions.question:
Consider socket API on a Linux machine that supports connected UDP sockets. A connected UDP socket is a UDP socket on which connect function has already been called. Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT?
I. A connected UDP socket can be used to communicate with multiple peers simultaneously.
II. A process can successfully call connect function for an already connected UDP socket.
A. I only
B. II only
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II
questions.to_find: Which statements about connected UDP sockets are correct

understand.plain: A connected UDP socket has had connect() called on it, which associates the socket with a specific remote address and port. This means it can only send/receive from that one peer (not multiple). However, unlike TCP, calling connect() again on an already-connected UDP socket is valid and reassociates it with a new peer, or even dissolves the association.

keywords.explain: A UDP socket on which connect() has been called, binding it to a specific (IP, port) peer address for sends and receives.
keywords.example: After connect(sock, &server_addr), send() goes only to server_addr.

keywords.explain: Does not establish a real connection (no handshake), but stores the peer address in the socket so subsequent send/recv use it by default. Can be called again to change or remove the peer association.
keywords.example: Calling connect() a second time with a different address re-associates the socket to the new peer.

understand.visual_alt: A connected UDP socket points to exactly one peer at a time. Re-calling connect() changes that peer. Unlike unconnected UDP, it cannot simultaneously address multiple peers.

given.aim: Evaluate two statements about connected UDP sockets in the Linux socket API.

terms.meaning: Claims a connected UDP socket can communicate with multiple peers simultaneously.
terms.example: Sending to peer A and peer B at the same time.
terms.connects: FALSE — a connected UDP socket is associated with exactly one remote address; all sends go to that address and only datagrams from that address are received.

terms.meaning: Claims connect() can be called again on an already-connected UDP socket.
terms.connects: TRUE — POSIX allows calling connect() multiple times on a UDP socket. The second call updates (or removes) the peer association.

terms.meaning: Unconnected UDP (no connect called) uses sendto()/recvfrom() with per-call addresses; connected UDP uses send()/recv() with the fixed peer.
terms.example: sendto() can specify any destination each call; send() on connected socket always goes to the connected peer.
terms.connects: Statement I is false because a connected socket loses the ability to communicate with arbitrary peers.

given.plan: Evaluate Statement I (FALSE: connected UDP restricts to one peer) and Statement II (TRUE: connect() is callable again on UDP to re-associate). Conclude only II is correct.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I
steps.apply: A connected UDP socket stores a fixed remote IP and port. The kernel uses this for all sends and filters incoming datagrams to accept only from this address. It CANNOT communicate with multiple peers simultaneously — it is associated with exactly one peer at a time. Statement I is FALSE.
steps.note: To communicate with multiple peers, one would need an unconnected UDP socket using sendto() with different addresses.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II
steps.apply: According to POSIX (and Linux socket API), calling connect() on an already-connected UDP socket is legal. It either reassociates the socket to a new remote address or, if given an address with AF_UNSPEC family, dissolves the association. Statement II is TRUE.
steps.note: This behavior is unique to UDP; for TCP, connect() on an already-connected socket returns an error (EISCONN).

solution.result: Statement I is FALSE, Statement II is TRUE. Answer: B. II only.

questions.formula_note: Connected UDP socket semantics: one-peer association via connect(); re-calling connect() on UDP is valid (unlike TCP); simultaneous multi-peer communication requires unconnected UDP with sendto().

questions.question:
A program on machine X attempts to open a UDP connection to port 5376 on a machine Y, and a TCP connection to port 8022 on machine Z. However, there are no applications listening at the corresponding ports on Y and Z. An ICMP Port Unreachable error will be generated by
A. Y but not Z
B. Z but not Y
C. Neither Y nor Z
D. Both Y and Z
questions.to_find: Which machine generates an ICMP Port Unreachable error when no application is listening

understand.plain: When a UDP datagram arrives at a host and no application is listening on the destination port, the OS generates an ICMP Port Unreachable message back to the sender. When a TCP connection attempt arrives and no application is listening, the OS sends back a TCP RST (reset) segment — NOT an ICMP message. So only the UDP target (Y) generates ICMP Port Unreachable.

keywords.explain: An ICMP error message (type 3, code 3) sent when a UDP datagram arrives at a host but no process is listening on that port.
keywords.example: nmap UDP scan receives ICMP Port Unreachable when the port is closed.

keywords.explain: A TCP segment with the RST flag set, sent by the OS when a TCP SYN arrives for a port with no listening application. This is a TCP-level response, not ICMP.
keywords.example: Trying to telnet to a closed port returns 'Connection refused' due to RST.

markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#B4B2A9"/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Machine X sends UDP to Y:5376 (no listener) — Y responds with ICMP Port Unreachable. Machine X sends TCP SYN to Z:8022 (no listener) — Z responds with TCP RST, not ICMP.

given.aim: Determine which machine(s) generate an ICMP Port Unreachable error when no application is listening on the target port.

terms.meaning: X sends a UDP datagram to Y:5376; Y has no application listening on that port.
terms.example: Like sending a DNS query to a server that has no DNS service running.
terms.connects: No UDP listener -> OS on Y generates ICMP Port Unreachable (type 3, code 3) back to X.

terms.meaning: X sends a TCP SYN to Z:8022; Z has no application listening on that port.
terms.example: Like trying to SSH to a server that has SSH disabled.
terms.connects: No TCP listener -> OS on Z sends TCP RST back to X. No ICMP is generated.

terms.meaning: ICMP error generated specifically for UDP datagrams arriving at a closed port.
terms.example: Tools like nmap use this response to detect closed UDP ports.
terms.connects: Only applies to UDP — TCP uses its own RST mechanism for rejected connections.

given.plan: UDP closed port -> ICMP Port Unreachable from Y. TCP closed port -> TCP RST from Z (no ICMP). Therefore only Y generates ICMP Port Unreachable.

steps.title: Analyze the UDP datagram to Y:5376
steps.apply: X sends a UDP datagram to Y on port 5376. Y's IP stack receives it, looks up port 5376 in its UDP socket table, finds no listening socket, and per RFC 792 generates an ICMP Destination Unreachable — Port Unreachable (type 3, code 3) message back to X. So Y DOES generate ICMP Port Unreachable.
steps.note: ICMP Port Unreachable is the standard response for UDP datagrams to closed ports.

steps.title: Analyze the TCP SYN to Z:8022
steps.apply: X sends a TCP SYN to Z on port 8022. Z's TCP stack receives the SYN, finds no listening socket on port 8022, and responds with a TCP RST+ACK segment directly in TCP. This is a Layer-4 TCP response. No ICMP message is generated by Z. So Z does NOT generate ICMP Port Unreachable.
steps.note: TCP handles closed-port notification internally via RST; ICMP is not involved in normal TCP rejection.

solution.result: Y (UDP) generates ICMP Port Unreachable; Z (TCP) sends TCP RST instead. Answer: A. Y but not Z.

questions.formula_note: ICMP Port Unreachable (RFC 792, type 3 code 3) is generated for UDP datagrams arriving at closed ports; TCP uses RST segment for the same scenario — no ICMP is involved.
